frontmatter.title: 6. AI and sound
frontmatter.subtitle: Speech, music, and sound design with generative audio models

children.value: Sound used to be the laggard medium for generative AI. Images and text arrived in 2022; high-quality audio took another year or two to catch up. By 2024 we had usable text-to-music systems and convincing voice cloning; by 2026 these are everywhere from podcast workflows to film production to
. This week we look at what these systems can actually do, and where they still fail.
The audio chapter is also where the question of
becomes most personal: voices are tied to bodies, and audio cloning of a real person can be used to harm.
Three quick families of audio AI

children.label: Three quick families of audio AI

children.value: You will meet at least three quite different things under the umbrella of “AI and sound”:

Synthetic voices reading text. Quality has been transformed by neural vocoders and now by transformer-based systems. Tools: ElevenLabs, Resemble, OpenAI TTS, Microsoft VALL-E, Norwegian-language efforts like
Systems that produce full pieces of music from text prompts. Tools: Suno, Udio, Stable Audio, MusicLM in research form
Generative SFX for film/game pipelines: footsteps on different surfaces, ambient atmospheres, Foley.

Underneath all of them sit the same core ideas as image generation: a model trained on huge amounts of audio learns to predict, and at inference time produces, audio that resembles its training distribution.

children.alt: A schematic spectrogram with frequency on the y-axis and time on the x-axis.

children.value: A spectrogram represents sound as an image (time on x, frequency on y). Many audio models treat sound generation as image generation on a spectrogram.
Computers store sound as a sequence of numbers — typically 44 100 or 48 000 of them per second per channel. That is a lot. Generating sound directly
(as the first WaveNet model did in 2016) was beautiful but slow.
Modern audio models almost always work on a
— a 2D image of the sound (time × frequency). Treat it as an image, diffuse over it, then convert back to audio with a
(like EnCodec, SoundStream, DAC). The model produces a short sequence of codes; the codec decodes them back to audio.
Both approaches make the problem 50–100× smaller than working on raw samples. This is why your laptop can now produce a song in seconds.
The architectural backbone is again usually a
, adapted to handle the very long sequences that audio implies. Diffusion models on spectrograms or codec tokens are common
describing the desired voice, possibly extracted from a short reference recording (5–30 seconds is often enough),
in English and most major European languages, including Norwegian (with the right model).
via prompts (“sad, slow”), tags, or a reference clip.
— one voice speaking many languages without re-recording.
— a 20-minute audiobook can drift in tone.
in TTS engines (separate music systems do better).
, especially with code or technical terms.
— Sami, Faroese, many African and Asian languages still get poor results.
You can clone a voice in two minutes from a YouTube clip. This is a fact, not a recommendation.
Cloning a real person’s voice without their consent is harmful and, increasingly, illegal
— for fraud, for harassment, for impersonation. Treat voice cloning as you would treat using somebody’s face: with explicit permission, attribution where appropriate, and a clear use case.
European Parliament,Council of the European Union, 2024
places certain forms of deepfake audio in the higher-risk categories with transparency obligations.
Music generation is harder than speech because:
— wrong notes are not “wrong” in the same way as wrong words.
— music is heavily copyrighted; using it for training has triggered lawsuits.
Despite all that, by 2026 tools like
will reliably produce 2–3 minute songs from a paragraph of prompt. Stems can often be separated for further editing in a DAW.
Useful prompt elements for music models:
: “fingerpicked acoustic guitar, brushed snare, double bass”.
, when supported, in their own field with
Asking for “in the style of Taylor Swift” raises legal and ethical alarms and is often blocked.
in languages other than English. Norwegian-language music output is improving but uneven.
beyond pop forms — fugues, multi-section classical works, free-jazz dialogues.
Beyond speech and music sits a quieter category:
. Models that produce 5–15 seconds of “rain on a tin roof”, “wooden cart on cobblestones”, or “alien ambience” are reshaping film, game, and podcast workflows. Tools include
, Stable Audio, AudioGen, and various open-source efforts.
unsung workhorse: less spectacular than song generation, less morally fraught than voice cloning, often the most immediately useful in production.
Where sound AI fits in a real workflow

children.label: Where sound AI fits in a real workflow

children.value: AI is rarely the whole pipeline.
Generated audio gets imported into a DAW (Reaper, Ableton, Logic, Reason). It is layered, EQ-ed, mixed, and sometimes re-recorded.
and commercial offerings let you split a song into vocals, drums, bass, and other. This makes AI generation useful even when you cannot get clean stems out of it.
made transcription nearly free. Researchers, journalists, and podcasters use it daily. We will use it in the practice session.
Part 1 — Transcribe and remix a short clip (60 min)

children.label: Part 1 — Transcribe and remix a short clip (60 min)

children.value: Record (or pick) a 30–60 second voice clip with your permission to use.
(via the web, via a local install, or via a service).
reading the same text in a TTS tool.
Compare the two side by side. What did the AI catch? What did it miss? What does the synthetic voice add or remove?
Pick a small brief: “background music for a UiO research lab promo video, 30 seconds”.
Generate a song in a music tool. Iterate prompts until you have something usable.
Generate a separate ambient sound effect bed.
Mix the two (any DAW; even Audacity works).
Document: tools, prompts, edits, time taken.

pip install openai-whisper
whisper my-clip.mp3 --model small --language Norwegian
library by Meta lets you run MusicGen locally on a moderate GPU.
Voice cloning is now essentially a free service. What changes for journalism? For political ads? For your own digital footprint?
Listen carefully to a generated 30-second clip. What gives it away? What does
give it away? Are you sure?

Music generation models were trained on existing music. Imagine you are a working musician — how do you feel about that? Imagine you are a film composer working on a small project — how does that change your answer?
Neural Audio Synthesis of Musical Notes with WaveNet Autoencoders
— early but influential paper from Google’s Magenta team
project at the National Library of Norway — a great example of a low-resource-language AI effort
